saida_bruta:
As the boys climbed the hill, she noticed them hesitate and confer with one another. "You ask her, Niel."

"You'd better, George. She's used to seeing you around your house all the time, but she barely knows me well enough to speak to."

As they paused before the three steps leading up to the front porch, Mrs. Forrester appeared in the doorway and nodded graciously, a pink rose in her hand.

"Good morning, boys. Off on a picnic?"

George Adams stepped forward and solemnly removed his big straw hat. "Good morning, Mrs. Forrester. Might we fish and wade down in the marsh, and eat our lunch in the grove?"

"Certainly. What a lovely day for it. How long has school been out? Don't you miss it? I'm sure Niel does—Judge Pommeroy tells me he's quite studious."

The boys laughed, and Niel looked embarrassed.

"Run along now, and be sure not to leave the pasture gate open. Mr. Forrester can't bear to have the cattle get into his blue grass."

The boys made their way quietly around the house to the gate leading into the grove, then dashed down the grassy slopes beneath the tall trees, shouting as they went. Mrs. Forrester watched them from the kitchen window until they vanished behind the rise of the hill. Then she turned to her Bohemian cook.

"Mary, while you're baking this morning, set aside a pan of cookies for those boys. I'll bring them down when they stop for lunch."

George Adams stepped forward and gravely lifted his big straw hat. "Good morning, Mrs. Forrester. Might we fish and wade down in the marsh, and eat our lunch in the grove?"

"Of course you may. What a fine day for it! How long has school been out? Don't you miss it already? I know Niel must—Judge Pommeroy tells me he's quite the scholar."

The boys slipped quietly around the house to the gate that opened into the grove, then went tumbling down the grassy slopes beneath the tall trees, shouting as they ran. Mrs. Forrester watched them from the kitchen window until they vanished over the rise of the hill. Then she turned to her Bohemian cook.

"Mary, while you're baking this morning, set aside a pan of cookies for those boys. I'll carry them down when the boys stop to eat their lunch."

saida_bruta:
As the boys made their way up the hill, she saw them falter, whispering back and forth.

"You ask her, Niel."

"No, you do it, George. She's used to seeing you around the house, but she barely knows me well enough for me to speak up."

When they reached the three steps leading up to the porch, Mrs. Forrester appeared in the doorway, a pink rose in hand, and greeted them warmly.

"Good morning, boys. Off on a picnic, are you?"

George Adams stepped forward and gravely raised his big straw hat. "Good morning, Mrs. Forrester. Might we fish and wade in the marsh, and have our lunch in the grove?"

"Certainly you may. What a beautiful day for it! How long has school been out now? I expect you already miss it—especially you, Niel. Judge Pommeroy tells me you're quite the scholar."

The boys burst out laughing, and Niel flushed with embarrassment.

"Off you go, then—and mind you close the pasture gate behind you. Mr. Forrester can't abide the cattle getting into his blue grass."

The boys slipped quietly around the house to the gate that opened into the grove, then went tumbling down the grassy slopes beneath the tall trees, shouting as they ran. Mrs. Forrester watched from the kitchen window until they disappeared over the crest of the hill. Then she turned to her Bohemian cook.

"Mary, while you're baking this morning, set aside a pan of cookies for those boys. I'll take them down when they stop to eat their lunch."
saida_filtrada:
As the boys made their way up the hill, she saw them falter, whispering back and forth.

"You ask her, Niel."

"No, you do it, George. She's used to seeing you around the house, but she barely knows me well enough for me to speak up."

When they reached the three steps leading up to the porch, Mrs. Forrester appeared in the doorway, a pink rose in hand, and greeted them warmly.

"Good morning, boys. Off on a picnic, are you?"

George Adams stepped forward and gravely raised his big straw hat. "Good morning, Mrs. Forrester. Might we fish and wade in the marsh, and have our lunch in the grove?"

"Certainly you may. What a beautiful day for it! How long has school been out now? I expect you already miss it—especially you, Niel. Judge Pommeroy tells me you're quite the scholar."

The boys burst out laughing, and Niel flushed with embarrassment.

"Off you go, then—and mind you close the pasture gate behind you. Mr. Forrester can't abide the cattle getting into his blue grass."

The boys slipped quietly around the house to the gate that opened into the grove, then went tumbling down the grassy slopes beneath the tall trees, shouting as they ran. Mrs. Forrester watched from the kitchen window until they disappeared over the crest of the hill. Then she turned to her Bohemian cook.

"Mary, while you're baking this morning, set aside a pan of cookies for those boys. I'll take them down when they stop to eat their lunch."
